announcements.title: Pet Care Donations - Support the Humane Society
announcements.body:
PEP is collecting donations for the Associated Humane Society – Newark Shelter (
https://ahscares.org/newark/
) from
Monday, March 16 through Friday, March 20
. Please drop off unopened, unexpired dog, cat, or kitten food, as well as clean blankets and towels, in the donation bins outside of SOCDS.
The shelter recently launched an exciting new program called Adventure Tails, where volunteers can take a dog out of the shelter for a few hours. If you'd like to sign up, you can do so here
https://www.shelterluv.com/form/volunteer/AHNJ/79774-adventure-tails-program-interest-form
.
If you're interested in helping transport donations from the SOMA community to the shelter in the future, please contact Sara Paddock directly at
sara.paddock@gmail.com
. They’re always looking for extra hands!

announcements.title: Five in a Row
announcements.body: Our books this month celebrate a variety of spring or renewal themes.

announcements.title: Ramadan Moon
announcements.body: This story introduces children to the month of Ramadan and the relationship to the moon.

announcements.title: Leprechaun's Treasure
announcements.body: As we build Leprechaun traps, this story emphasizes kindness, empathy, and the meaning of luck.

announcements.title: Festival of Colors
announcements.body: This book follows a family through their preparations for Holi and the use of color to spread spring joy and hope.

announcements.title: I am Love
announcements.body:
This book encourages children to sit with their upset and look inward with love and affection for themselves and others.

Licensing Update
This week there was a

announcements.title: New York Times
announcements.body:
article describing health and safety violations at a Bright Horizons center in New York City. At SOCDS, we are deeply honored by the trust you place in us each day to care for your children, and we work hard to earn and maintain that trust.
While I have sometimes taken a critical view of our licensing agency because their standards can feel overly focused on small details rather than the quality of teacher-child interactions, they do play an important role in maintaining public confidence in the childcare industry. At SOCDS, we recognize that we are not perfect. When mistakes happen, we work to address them thoughtfully, adjust our processes, and remain transparent and clear with our
families.
We rely on you—our partners—to let us know when something doesn’t feel right, or when your child shares something that is confusing or out of line with your expectations. Your willingness to communicate with us helps us grow, improve, and ensure that every child receives the safest, most nurturing preschool experience possible.
This year SOCDS is due for our three (3) year renewal.  Our DCF Inspector, Tina (we refer to her as "the women with the clipboard") will review all our processes, paperwork, and building safety.  We have passed our lead-free interior and water tests, as well as affirming that we are Radon free.  DCF visits are not scheduled and occur every six months at a minimum.  If you have
any questions or concerns, please let me know.
Ramadan Moon Lighting Event
Sunday 5pm
Please join our neighbors as they begin the season of Ramadan.  Maplewood is hosting a moon lighting event at the Springfield Gazebo at 5pm on Sunday.

announcements.title:
Half Way - Week #20
We are officially halfway through the school year, and it’s a wonderful moment to pause and reflect on just how much your children have grown — emotionally and socially.  These months of shared routines, language, and experiences are what build that foundation of trust and connection — and it shows in the way your children move through their days with increasing independence and empathy.
We see growing confidence, stronger self-advocacy, deeper friendships, and a beautiful sense of responsibility toward one another. Our classrooms truly feel like communities now, where children look out for each
other, solve problems together, and take pride in belonging.
As part of this mid-year reflection and partnership, we will be scheduling
Zoom parent teacher conferences for the end of February
. These conversations are an important opportunity to connect, share observations, celebrate growth, and talk together about how best to continue supporting your child’s development through the rest of the year. More details and sign-up information will be coming
soon. - Music Presentations
announcements.body:
As your children have likely shared, each class is exploring different musical genres and preparing a “museum-style” experience to share with the rest of the school. In December, we tried to do all of the sharing in one day, and it felt a bit rushed — and like a lot to take in!
For our

announcements.title: Music Museums
announcements.body:
, we’ll spread the visits out over the week. This will give everyone time to truly enjoy each presentation and allow every child the space to share their ideas, interests, and favorites with their schoolmates.
Thank you for your partnership and support.

announcements.title: Inclement Weather
announcements.body:
The weekend weather might spread into Monday.
South Orange Country Day School follows the lead of the South Orange–Maplewood Public School District when it comes to weather-related closures. If the South Orange–Maplewood schools are closed due to inclement
weather, we will also be closed. If the district announces a delayed opening (of any duration), SOCDS will open at
10:00 AM
for all students.
I will also send an eblast as soon as I learn of the decision.
As always, preschool attendance is not mandatory. If you feel your child would be safer staying home or being picked up early, please do whatever you feel is best—just be sure to let us know your
plans.
If you have any questions or concerns please text (1-646-226-8790) or email learn@socds.com.

announcements.title: 5 in a Row - January and February
announcements.body:
As explained last week, our themes and school celebrations are very connected in January and February.  These books allow us to nurture our classroom and school community with a focus on inclusion.  This goes beyond accepting differences to celebrating and integrating differences.
We have combined the 5 in a Row books together as well.  I have included a read-aloud below for your reference.

announcements.title: Happy Birthday Martin Luther King
announcements.body:
This book summarizes the life of the civil rights leader for children, highlighting his Atlanta childhood, becoming a pastor, leading peaceful protests against segregation (like bus & school segregation), his dream of equality ("I Have a Dream"), and his tragic assassination, all to explain why his birthday is a national holiday celebrating peace and brotherhood
.

announcements.title: Mae Among the Stars
announcements.body:
Tells the story of a young girl's dream to travel to space, emphasizing how her parents' encouragement and her own hard work helped her overcome obstacles (like a skeptical teacher) to become the first African American woman in space.
A Computer Called Katherine
Tells the inspiring true story of mathematician

announcements.title: Katherine Johnson
announcements.body: , a math prodigy who overcame racial and gender barriers to become a vital "human computer" at NASA, calculating trajectories for America's first spaceflights, including Alan Shepard's orbit and John Glenn's Earth orbit, ultimately helping land the first man on the Moon.

announcements.title: If I Only Had a Horn
announcements.body: A wonderful journey through the life of Louie Armstrong and his desire to share his music.

announcements.title: Saving the Day
announcements.body:
: Garrett Morgan's Life Changing Invention of the Traffic Signal
This story tells the story of how and why Garrett Morgan invented the traffic signal, and the struggles he had along the way.

announcements.title: Your Name is a Song
announcements.body: This book explores the beauty of the names our families gift us and reminds us all to respect and appreciate thenames of our friends - even if we have not heard them before.

announcements.title: A Piece of Home
announcements.body: Explores a families travels to a new community and how they share their own traditions and join in learning about others too

announcements.title: My name is Yoon
announcements.body:
This book beautiful describes the beautiful the Korean script (the letters in my name look like dancing birds) and how different the feeling is in English script.

announcements.title: Holiday Wishes
announcements.body:
As the year comes to a close, we wish your family a joyful holiday filled with togetherness, warmth, and meaningful moments.
Thank you for sharing your children with us—we hope this season brings time to reconnect and enjoy one another.
Extended - PEP Family Traditions

announcements.title: Collection
announcements.body:

announcements.title: Naturalists Museum
announcements.body:
This week, the classes are wrapping up their naturalist study and preparing to share their work with the rest of the school next week.  We used this opportunity to learn about different regions of America and explore how the land, plants, and animals vary from place to place.
The students learned about how these scientists helped protect the environment, animals, and their ecosystems. They also learned that even people who do important and helpful things may not always be inclusive or thoughtful in every part of their lives.
George Washington Carver - PreK Gray & Youngsters
George Washington Carver showed people how to take care of the soil and grow crops in better ways, which helped farmers all over the country. He also created new uses for plants like peanuts and sweet potatoes, inspiring others to think creatively about science and nature.
Theodore Roosevelt - PreK Purple
Theodore Roosevelt helped protect huge areas of land, forests, and wildlife by creating many National Parks and wildlife refuges. He believed nature should be saved for everyone to enjoy, and his actions helped start the conservation movement in the United States.
Jane Goodall - PreK Orange
Jane Goodall taught the world how important it is to care for animals by studying chimpanzees in their natural habitat. Her work helped people understand that animals have feelings, families, and needs, and she inspired others to protect wildlife and the environment.
John Muir - PreK Green
John Muir helped people understand the importance of protecting nature. His writing and work inspired the creation of National Parks, helping to save beautiful places, forests, and animals for everyone to enjoy.
Rachel Carson - Ms. Patricia Kindergarten
Rachel Carson helped people understand that some chemicals were harming animals and the environment. Her work encouraged everyone to take better care of nature and helped start the modern environmental movement.
Hazel Johnson - Ms. Trudi's Kindergarten
Hazel Johnson worked to make sure all families—especially those in neglected neighborhoods—had clean air, clean water, and safe places to live. Her efforts helped people understand that every community deserves a healthy environment, and she inspired many others to speak up for environmental justice.

announcements.title: A gentle reminder: what
announcements.body:
children appreciate most during this busy season is the time they spend with you working and laughing together.
Try to simplify your traditions to the ones that hold the most meaning for you and reflect the values and memories you hope to pass on. When you explain to your children why these traditions matter, you help them deepen their understanding of what it means to be part of a family and strengthen those traditions for the future. And don’t be afraid to make new ones too!

announcements.body:
Thank you!
Thank you to all our students and families for the lovely cards and treats. We so appreciate your kind words and beautiful pictures.
Thank you to PEP for organizing a teacher lunch on Thursday — we were absolutely full and happy! And thank you to everyone who brought in bagels, teas, cupcakes, and doughnuts throughout the week. Your generosity truly lifted our spirits.
A special acknowledgement to PEP for coordinating the Thanksgiving placement coloring project. Our students worked so thoughtfully on their
placemats, and Ms. Jenny and the other teachers discussed the importance of helping others and how small acts of kindness can spread joy throughout our community.  These will be distributed through the OLS Thanksgiving Food Distribution this weekend.  The first two pictures above show Luca delivering the placements and the vast food organization required to serve the need in our community.  Thank you all.

sections.gardening:
We did a lot of work this week, creating a new garden space around the oak tree at the entrance of our school. After spreading topsoil and compost we planted small Anemone blanda bulbs. Hopefully, they will yield little daisy-like, blue flowers in April and May.
We were also busy clearing dead plants out of our garden beds, putting the stalks in the compost and aerating the pile.  The kale is still thriving and we were
able to harvest enough of it to make kale chips for next week’s Thanksgiving treat.
Before the holiday break, all the children will have a chance to bring home a purple coneflower seed head along with planting instructions. Coneflowers or Echinacea are native to North America and are an important source of nectar for butterflies and birds. The seeds can be planted now (they require cold winter temperatures before they are able to germinate in the spring). Through this effort, we hope to encourage the spread of pollinator-friendly plants throughout the community.

sections.gardening:
Over the past two weeks we have planted over 125 bulbs. Except for the tulips, all the bulbs have been selected because they are deer deterrent. The snowdrops (Galanthus) will bloom first, as early as February, followed by tulips, daffodils, winter windflower (Anemone blanda) and summer
snowflakes (Leucojum aestivum) by mid-spring. Rising 32 inches in height and adored by bees, butterflies and other pollinators, Allium purple sensation is the last of our bulbs to bloom in late spring or early summer. It has been challenging to dig and plant bulbs as the temperatures drop but we stay warm by keeping busy. We’ve been raking leaves and adding them to the compost bin to achieve a balance between “brown” and “green” materials.  It was exciting to chop up a pumpkin and examine the fungi that was growing on the inside before feeding it to the “decomposers” in the bin.
PEP Thanksgiving Placements
OLS Thanksgiving Food Distribution
Thank you to our wonderful SOCDS PEP team for organizing a meaningful opportunity for our students to spread caring throughout our community. Mary Payne (Ruby’s mom) created the beautiful placement design above, and Rachel Pattison (Luca’s mom) has coordinated the distribution to the OLS Thanksgiving Food Drive. Next week, the children will color their placements in class, and we will laminate them before they are shared.
Doing for others is a powerful way for children to understand empathy, generosity, and the impact of small acts of kindness.
Children will not sign their placements, and there will be no identification of where they came from in
order to protect our students.

sections.art:
Ms. Jenny introduced the children to use tape and paint to create contrast.
Children used tape to outline the shape of a tree on paper before painting over the entire surface. Once the paint dries, the tape is gently peeled away to reveal clean white lines where the tape had been—creating the trunk and branches of the tree.
This process teaches children about positive and negative space, helps develop fine motor skills, and produces striking, high-contrast artwork that highlights both color and form.
sections.gardening:
This week’s weather conditions were perfect to explore the survival adaptations of the swamp milkweed (Asclepius incarnata). Their seeds are snugly packed into 4-inch pods. Each seed has soft, white filaments attached at their base that look like fairy wings or a parachute that
allows the seed to float up and ride on a wind current or attach itself to an animal’s fur. The children had a joyful experience running and blowing them into the wind.  We hope that the seeds will germinate and grow milkweed all over the garden.  Monarch butterflies are especially attracted to milkweed because it is the sole food source for their caterpillars and the only plant (in all its varieties) where they will lay their eggs. Adult monarchs also feed on the plant’s nectar to fuel their migration, and the toxic compounds in its sap protect them from predators.

announcements.title: Tabby and Tommy Cat - Inclusion
announcements.body:
We are continuing to talk about consent and the importance of asking a friend
before
touching them or their belongings. We are also helping children practice both language and body signals they can use when a friend forgets to ask. For example:
“I am your friend, but please ask me before you touch my stuffy.”
(Hands up in a stop motion) “Please stop touching my hat. Please ask me before you touch it.”
We are also introducing the concept of Inclusion and
how to invite friends to join your play.  We discuss how our body language also sends cues to our friends.

sections.art:

sections.gardening:
We had a delivery of 1.5 cubic yards of topsoil and compost last Saturday. The children in each class took turns loading the wheelbarrow to deliver this rich soil to amend our garden plots. Many
of the warm weather crops we grew last summer are heavy feeders including corn, tomatoes, peppers and cucumbers. To prepare for the next growing season we must add nutrients back into the soil. To keep our garden beds healthy we cleared them of dried stalks and leaves, whenever possible. However, we were careful to leave the seedpods intact for the wildlife that shares our ecosystem.

announcements.title: Walking Field Trip to the Firehouse - - Great Success
announcements.body:
Your children were so excited to walk to the firehouse and meet the firefighters! They did an excellent job walking with their partners, listening carefully to their teachers, and staying safe together.
At the firehouse, the firefighters spoke about fire safety and demonstrated their equipment. They even put on their full gear to show the children what they look like during an emergency—reminding everyone not to be afraid if a firefighter ever comes into their home to help.

to

sections.gardening:
We’ve been enjoying the crisp fall weather as we continue our study of trees. We learned that there are many kinds of maple trees. We looked carefully at the leaves of three common varieties that are growing at SOCDS: Sugar, Red and Japanese maple trees. We also compared two types of Oak trees (Pin Oak and Black Oak) by comparing the shape
of their leaves, trunks, branching habits and seeds. We learned that Oak trees are the superheroes of the tree world, in fact they are a keystone species. In North America, they support more life forms including fungi, insects, birds and mammals than any other tree genus. We also looked at several tree seedlings in the garden and discussed how their thin stems can grow year after year into a huge tree trunk.
It is chilly!
Please dress your child appropriately to be outside for 30 minutes at a time.  Good foot wear and a warm hat are useful as many children find heavier jackets constraining.

sections.gardening:
The leaves on our sugar maple trees are starting to change color and drop to the ground. We learned that sugar maples belong to a
group of trees that drop their leaves before they go dormant in winter. They are classified as deciduous trees in contrast to our eastern hemlock tree (next to the front entrance of our school) which is evergreen and is classified as a conifer. We started our observations by looking closely at the shape of the sugar maple leaves which are almost as tall as they are wide. They typically have 5 lobes with moderately deep U-shaped notches (sinuses) between lobes. We counted at least 10 sugar maple trees growing at the school so it was easy to find leaves to examine. We compared them to the needle-shaped leaves of the eastern hemlock which have evolved to withstand harsh winters allowing them to stay green all year and retain water and nutrients.
We also
did some work to clear out our garden beds to prepare for winter. We pulled out a tomato plant and two pepper plants because they won’t have a chance to bear fruit before the first frost date. Both plants are sun-loving, hot weather crops. We were not surprised to see that their fibrous roots were 1.5-2 feet deep and wide.

announcements.title: Getting Ready to Celebrate - America's 250th Year
announcements.body:
As our country prepares to celebrate the 250th anniversary of its founding next July, we want to provide children with many opportunities to think about what it means to be an American.
Because the children are already familiar with maps and flags as symbols for holidays and cultural recognitions, we will build on this knowledge by exploring American maps from both the past and the present. We will use age-appropriate language, but we will not shy away from discussing difficult truths—much like the way we talk about the murder of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.
These conversations will begin this fall and continue through July 2026, giving us time to engage with a variety of ideas and reflect on the promise of
America.
To start, we will learn Woody Guthrie’s song

announcements.title: This Land Is Your Land
announcements.body:
. This work will likely take us several months, and it will connect beautifully to our map explorations while also helping us expand vocabulary and deepen our sense of shared belonging.
If you have any thoughts, ideas, or concerns, please share them with us. This is a journey we are taking together, and we deeply value your family’s experiences, insights, and even worries along the way.
This Land Is Your Land
This land is your land, and this land is my land
From California to the New York Island
From the Redwood Forest to the Gulf Stream Waters
This land was made for you and me
As I went walking that ribbon of highway
And I saw above me that endless skyway
I saw below me that golden valley
This land was made for you and me
I roamed and rambled and I followed my footsteps
To the sparkling sands of her diamond deserts
All around me a voice was a-sounding
This land was made for you and me
There was a big high wall there that tried to stop me
Sign was painted, said, "Private Property"
But on the back side, it didn't say nothing
This land was made for you and me
When the sun comes shining, then I was strolling
And the wheat fields waving and the dust clouds rolling
A voice come chanting as the fog was lifting
This land was made for you and me
This land is your land, and this land is my land
From California to the New York Island
From Redwood Forest to the Gulf Stream Waters
This land was made for you and me

sections.art:
This week the children explored the art of
Peter Anton
, a contemporary American artist best known for his giant, realistic sculptures of chocolates, candies, and—of course—donuts.
Inspired by his work, Ms. Jenny invited the children to experiment with clay, rolling, pinching, and sculpting their own creations. Thanks to the warm weather, the pieces dried quickly and were soon ready for the final touch—bright, cheerful paint colors.
sections.gardening:
The Fall season is the perfect time to begin our study of trees. We discussed the fact that trees are plants. They are among the largest and longest living plants on Earth. We talked about the many ways we depend on trees to give us food and other necessities such as paper and building materials. We learned that trees prevent soil erosion, produce oxygen, absorb carbon dioxide and provide habitats for countless species. Most importantly, trees give us shade on a hot day. We were amazed to discover that a tiny acorn could grow up to be as big as the mighty oak tree in the front yard of our school!
The Kindergarteners tasted the pickles they made last week and discussed the importance of knowing how to preserve the fruits and vegetables we harvest.

sections.gardening:
It’s officially Fall as we celebrate the changing of the seasons by looking carefully at how our garden has transformed in the past few weeks. Our herb garden was beginning to be overrun by grass and weeds. Their roots are strong and must be loosened by digging deep with a shovel before pulling them out. We learned to remove dried stalks by pressing down and then pulling them out. We cut back the tomato plants including one plant that germinated in the compost bin and pushed its way out a hole into the sunshine. We are clearing the beds of dried plant materials before we add a layer of compost and topsoil to prepare for winter. All this “green” material went into the compost bin as an important source of nitrogen. Next, we added water and aerated the pile with a pitchfork. Next week, we will add a layer of dried leaves (as a source of carbon) and
completed compost to feed the millions of “decomposers” that do the work of breaking down organic matter and turning it into soil or “black gold”.
Our Kindergarten children picked all the remaining cucumbers before pulling out the dried plants. To preserve the last of the harvest they made half-sour pickles using a salt brine, garlic, spices and herbs.

announcements.title: Five in a Row
announcements.body:
All of us at SOCDS truly feel that we have the best jobs in the world. Each day is filled with playing, laughing, learning, and reading alongside your children. They arrive eager for new experiences and hungry to learn.
Throughout the year, we use a series of books to spark curiosity and inspire connections. These shared stories create a common thread across the school—and the themes often find their way into imaginative play on the playground.
Most of the books focus on important social-emotional themes such as family, inclusion,
or feelings like being left out, angry, or scared.  We also use these books to celebrate holidays and cultural traditions.
Each class works with one book for an entire week in a program we call

announcements.title: Five in a Row
announcements.body:
. Early in the week, teachers guide children through the story itself—exploring the characters, themes, and even the author. Hearing the story multiple times gives all children an opportunity to make connections and build comprehension skills.  Once the children are comfortable with the content, teachers introduce a variety of extension activities inspired by the book, ranging from art and science to geography and more.
All classes will experience each of these books over the following six weeks through the end of October:
Abuela
. by Arthur Dorros
A grandmother and grand daughter have imaginative adventures across the city.
Vocabulary: abuela, flock, airport, city, harbor
Lots of Feelings
. by Shelley Rotner
Exploring a variety of emotions and body language.
Vocabulary: Shy, proud, frightened, curious
Engineer Ari and the Rosh Hashanah Ride
. by Deborah Bodin Cohen
Celebrate the holiday by spreading good wishes, while also remembering that our family and friends are treasures never to be taken for granted.

announcements.title: Vocabulary: challah, shofar, yeshivah, port city
announcements.body:
Can I play too?
by Mo Willems
Explore the difference between inclusion and "not-excluding."  Also practicing how to ask to join a group.
Vocabulary: throw, catch, wait, idea, problem solving
Let's Celebrate Diwali?
by Anjali Joshi
Learn about Diwali - the Festival of Lights.

announcements.title: Vocabulary: diya, lengha, kurtas, rangoli
announcements.body:
The Legend of Spookley - the square pumpkin?
by Joe Troiano
Being different and accepting each other for our own strengths.
Vocabulary: curves, round, square, bruised, sow

sections.art: Ms. Jenny started the still life painting projects.  Children were practicing with sunflowers and apples.
sections.gardening:
We are enjoying the warm, late summer days. This week we focused on seeds---they are all around us as some annual plants are coming to the end of their life cycles and perennials are dying back as they prepare to go dormant during the winter months. Some seeds provide us with essential nutrients that help us grow.  For example, we learned that corn kernels are seeds that may be eaten or dried and planted to grow new corn plants. Corn can be dried and ground to make corn meal used to make tacos or tortillas. We learned that wheat plants grow wheat berries that can be ground to make
flour that may be used to make bread, pasta, cake and even cookies. Other plants in the grass family are oats and rice. We discussed how seeds come in all different shapes and sizes. We held a coconut which was hard on the outside and quite heavy compared to a bean which is small, smooth; they are both seeds. Most important, we discovered that seeds look different yet they are all the same in one way: they each contain a tiny, tiny plant (embryo) inside.
We are beginning to clear out our raised beds. One of our cucumber plants was forming small, stunted cucumbers due to its dried and withered stem so we pulled it out and measured the vine which was 9 feet long. Then, we salvaged some seeds from dried pods on a string bean plant and discovered that its vine was 9 ½ feet long!

announcements.title: Walking in line and - Preparing for a Fire Drill
announcements.body:
This week, children have been practicing walking in a line with their classmates. Walking in a line helps us learn to move safely together as a group, whether we are heading outside, going to art, or enjoying gardening with Ms. Linda.  It gives children the chance to practice patience, awareness of personal space, and cooperation—skills that are important for everyday routines and for building a strong classroom community.
Next week, we will be preparing for our first
fire drill
of the school year. Fire drills are an important way for children to learn how to respond calmly and safely in case of an emergency. We practice walking in a line, listening carefully to teachers, and following directions so that everyone feels
confident and secure if we ever need to leave the building unexpectedly.
I will also announce the fire drills beforehand, and offer support to any child that has a particular concern.  The teachers have been explaining what to expect, answering questions, and providing children plenty of encouragement so they feel safe.
Morning Meeting
Our
noon playtime
is a special part of the day when all the classes are outside together. At other times, classes enjoy the playground at different times, but at noon the whole school shares the space, which gives children a chance to see
friends from other classrooms.
At
12:30pm
, we gather for

announcements.title: Morning Meeting
announcements.body: . Each class lines up with their teachers, and together we practice calming breathing exercises, sing our school songs, and recite our poem of the month. We also take time to celebrate by singing

announcements.title: Happy Birthday
announcements.body:
to our special friends!  We also share any important messages or stories to help us build a solid community and help friends make good choices.
Here are a few of those items:

announcements.title: Peace Begins with Me
announcements.body: We set our fingers with our thumb on our pinky.  We take a deep breath in, hold it, and then release, and move our thumb to each finger and recite "Peace Begins with Me."

announcements.title: Deep Breath and Hug Ourselves
announcements.body:
We raise our arms high, take a deep breath, and then give ourselves a squeeze.  On the exhale we say, "I am here.  I am safe.  I am loved."
Then, we spread our arms wide, take a deep breath, and then give ourselves a squeeze.  On the exhale we say, "We are here.  We are safe.  We are loved."
Magic Penny Song
Love is something if you give it away,
Give it away, give it away.
Love is something if you give it away,
You end up having more.
It's just like a magic penny,
Hold it tight and you won't have any.

announcements.title: Lend it, spend it, and you'll have so many
announcements.body:
They'll roll all over the floor.
For love is something if you give it away,
Give it away, give it away.
Love is something if you give it away,
You end up having more.
No bad weather,
just inappropriate clothing
SOCDS goes outside in all kinds of weather.  Please dress your child in appropriate weather gear so that they can enjoy the outside for up to 30 minutes.  Foot wear should be closed-toe so that children can run freely.

sections.art:
Ms. Jenny created a lesson on color and introduced the children to the artwork of
Alma Woodsey Thomas
. Thomas was a celebrated African American artist and the first African American woman to have a solo exhibition at the Whitney Museum in New York City.
Alma Woodsey Thomas was an abstract painter inspired by both nature and space. Her unique style featured
bright, vibrant colors arranged in mosaic-like patterns
, often forming circles or radiating bands.
The button below links to additional art work examples.

sections.gardening:
It’s exciting to begin the new school year. We began observations in the garden by identifying the plants that open their buds in late summer. The New England Aster has begun to bloom with purple petals and yellow composite centers. It is a native North American perennial. It is a valuable food source for late
season pollinators like bees and butterflies. We also looked closely at daisies and cosmos that are also in bloom. They are all members of the family Asteraceae, one of the largest and most diverse families of flowering plants. We then moved on to our vegetable garden to see if our carrots, string beans, tomatoes and cucumbers were ready to eat. Our last task was to determine if a few of our plants have come to the end of their life cycles. Our corn stalks were dried and ready to be pulled out. We observed that two cucumber plants could no longer support the growth of fully formed fruits because their stems were dry and withered. After pulling out the stems we studied the root systems of each plant.
Friday September 26th
Johnny Appleseed Activities
On Friday September 26th we will be celebrating Johnny Appleseed Day with a set of activities outside with the entire school. We are using these activity days to give children the chance to explore new experiences together to build a strong sense of community across our school.
If you have any creative ideas for age-appropriate activities, please let us know.  For now we have plans for an apple sorting, rolling apples (using red balls), Apple Tree Yoga, and apple tree "corn-hole."
For these first few activity days we are not seeking parent volunteers.  It is still difficult for several of our children to separate from parents and seeing
other adults can cause upset.  We will open these activities to families in the spring.
If you have any questions or concerns please text (1-646-226-8790) or email learn@socds.com.
Annemarie

announcements.title: Thank you
announcements.body:
We are deeply grateful that you have entrusted us with your children during these important early school years. They bring so much joy, curiosity, and energy to our school community, and their presence gives us great hope for the future.
If you weren’t able to attend the Back-to-School Zoom, please reach out and we’ll be happy to arrange a convenient time for you to connect with your child’s teacher.  We will organize parent teacher conferences in October, but please reach out with any questions
or concerns.
Driving and Parking
Please be sure to park on the
school side of Vose Avenue
. This helps reduce congestion and makes it safer for families entering and leaving the school.
Because Vose Avenue is a busy cut-through street, making K-turns and U-turns can be very dangerous. We ask that you take an extra minute to drive around the block so you can park safely on the school side.
We are fortunate to have wonderful neighbors on Vose Avenue. Please do not block any driveways when parking. Even if it’s “just for a
minute,” it creates an inconvenience and is disrespectful to our neighbors.

announcements.title: A few common questions:
announcements.body:
What is the best way to communicate changes to pick up schedule?
You can email me at
learn@socds.com
or text
1-646-226-8790
, and I will make sure to share your message with the teachers. You’re also welcome to send a note in with your child—teachers and children always find that exciting!
You are welcome to pick up your child at any time. A quick heads-up helps us prepare them for an easy and smooth transition.
What is the best way to notify the school that someone else is picking up your child?
Please send us the name and a photo of the person who will be picking up your child, and we will make sure your child knows who to expect.
If your child is hesitant or uncomfortable with that person, we will call you right away for guidance. For safety reasons, we do not release children to adults they do not feel comfortable with or to anyone who appears impaired.
If my child does enrichment, where are they at pick up?
Enrichment children remain in their classrooms until
4:45 pm
. If you are picking up before that time, please go directly to the building where your child’s classroom is located. The main house has a bell (please press for at least 5 seconds), and the Kindergarten group uses the door in the back of the carriage house.
After
4:45 pm
, children will be on the playground and their backpacks will be placed under the porch.
Please note that these times will shift with daylight changes, and we will communicate any updates with you.

sections.art:
Ms. Jenny introduced the children to the art studio.  There were conversations about art and representation, and students shared their favorite artists.
They also started their first self portraits of the year.  Ms. Jenny works on self portraits throughout the year to observe how children's view of themselves and their place in the world evolves.
